mnemonics.title: Rice's Theorem Reminder
mnemonics.content: RICE: 'Recursive languages, Invalid properties Cause Eternal confusion' - Any Non-trivial property of language recognized by TM is Undecidable

mnemonics.title: RE vs Recursive Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: RE = 'Running Eventually' (may loop forever) | Recursive = 'Runs Complete' (always halts) - Think of 'Recursive' as 'Re-curse removed' = always completes

mnemonics.title: Undecidable Problems List
mnemonics.content: UNDEcIDABLE: Universal TM problems, Non-trivial properties (Rice), Decidability of automata equivalence, Emptiness of TM language, Context-free problems (ambiguity), Intersection of CFLs, Diagonalization leads to contradictions, All reduction proofs end in undecidable

acronyms.usage: Abstract model of computation that defines what is computationally computable
Languages for which a TM exists that accepts all strings in L (may loop on others)
Undecidable problem used to prove undecidability of many other problems
Language where TM always halts (accepts or rejects)
Languages whose complement is RE - includes problems that are 'not RE'
Set of encodings (⟨M,w⟩) where TM M accepts w - this is RE but not recursive

visualTips.visualization: Draw three concentric circles: innermost = Regular (decidable), middle = Context-Free (some decidable), outermost = Type-0 (RE). Outside all = Undecidable. Show complement relationships with arrows.
Draw diagonal with two paths: 'Halts' and 'Loops'. Show how creating a machine that does opposite leads to paradox when applied to itself.

visualTips.visualize: Tree with trunk = 'Trivial properties (decidable)'. Branches = 'Non-trivial properties (undecidable)'. Leaves = specific problems like emptiness, equivalence, regularity.

analogies.analogy: Like asking 'Does this particular story have an ending?' - you can't know without reading forever. Some programs may run forever and you'll never know when to stop waiting.
A vending machine that takes your money and either gives product OR keeps your money forever (if it malfunctions). You only know success if it happens; failure never announces itself.

If you can solve B using A as a subroutine, and A is undecidable, then B must also be undecidable. Like: if you can build a perpetual motion machine using an impossible object, that object proves impossibility.

It's like saying: 'Can you determine if a friend's story has adventure in it without hearing the whole story?' - Any non-trivial property about what a TM 'means' is impossible to decide just from its code.
